content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

May 16, 1977

Mr. Ronald Kaldor
General Counsel
California Health Facilities Commission
555 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Kaldor:

     Thank you for your letter of March 18, 1977, providing
additional information with regard to the questions initially
raised in your letter of January 6, 1977. According to a recent
conversation with Mr. Ed Kerry of the Conflict-of-Interest Division, you are
withdrawing your second question concerning the State's obligation
to provide legal defense to members of the Health Facilities
Commission [Commission] accused of violating the requirements of
the Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended.

     In your first question, you inquire whether a physician,
who is a designated employee under the Conflict of Interest
Code of the Commission, has a financial interest in a hospital
under the following circumstances. The physician, acting as a
corporation, contracts with a hospital to provide service to
patients of the hospital. Income received by the corporation is
considered the professional fee for the service rendered to the
patient and not consideration from the contract with the hospital.
In the case of Radiology and Pathology, particularly, the contract
prevents any other physician from providing that service to patients
of that hospital. Virtually all of the physician's medical
income is received from the services rendered to hospital patients
under the contract with the corporation. Although the physician
is able to bring in patients for services, who are not patients
of the hospital, this is not usually done.

     The term "financial interest" is defined in Government Code
Section 87103, and sets forth the standards for compelling dis-
qualification under the Political Reform Act, Government Code
Section 87100. In order to have a "financial interest" in the
hospital, it must be reasonably foreseeable that a decision made
or participated in will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
uishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Mr. Ronald Kaldor
May 16, 1977
page two

"[a]ny source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is made;..."

At the onset it should be clear that income to an individual
includes a pro rata share of the income to a business entity in
which the individual owns a 10% interest or greater. Thus, where,
as appears here, the physician owns 100% of the corporation,
income to the corporation is income to the physician. With this
understanding, the first issue presented is whether the hospital
is a source of income to the physician or whether the physician's
source of income is his patients? While it is true, under the
recited facts, that the physician's income is received from
patients served by the physician and not from the hospital itself,
it is also quite clear that the patients are hospital patients,
that the facilities used to provide the services are hospital
facilities, and that absent the physician's contractual relationship with the hospital, the physician would have other or no
facilities to use, other or no patients to serve and would receive
income via other sources. Thus, the physician clearly is dependent
for his or her income on the operation of the hospital. Under
these circumstances, it is our view that both the hospital and
the patients are a source of income to the physician.

You next ask:

"If that financial interest is required to be reported by
the Conflict of Interest Code, is the value of the interest:

1)  the income received as a result of the contract
    with the hospital;

2)  the income received by the corporation for pro-
    fessional services rendered at the hospital; or

3)  the portion of the income in 2) which is earned by
    the doctor designated in the Conflict of Interest
    Code?"

The value of the source of income, which is the hospital, is
difficult to determine, but must be based not on the amounts of
money received from hospital patients, but rather upon the approximate value of the contract between the hospital and the corporation.

Mr. Ronald Kaldor
May 16, 1977
page three

Monetary value is inherent and actual in the context of this
contractual relationship. As a practical matter, this should
pose no disclosure problem even in a code, since if the approximate contract value to the corporation is in excess of $10,000,
the hospital must be listed as a source of income to the corporation
without the assignment of greater definitional value. (See,
Government Code Section 87207(b)(3)).

If the physician were not incorporated, the physician
would be required to report each source of income of $250 or
more received by the physician. However, based upon our understanding of the disclosure requirements of a code for the
Commission, patients would not have to be reported, since they
could not be materially financially affected by Commission
decisions.

I hope that this informal advice will be of some assistance
to you in advising the members of your Commission and in preparing
your Conflict of Interest Code.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division
(916)322-6444

DLS:mfa

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

CALIFORNIA HEALTH FACILITIES COMMISSION
555 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-2810

March 18, 1977


Mr. Ed Kerry
Fair Political Practices Commission
925 J Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, California  95814


Dear Ed:

     Earlier this year I requested legal opinions on two questions
affecting the California Health Facilities Commission and its
Proposed Conflict of Interest Code.

     The first concerned the kind of reportable financial interest
that a physician would have in a hospital depending on the kind
of contractual relations that he or his corporation had with
that hospital. It also concerned how the value of that interest
would be determined. The additional information that you requested
is the specific members of the medical profession that we wish to
protect by this opinion. They are E. Kash Rose, M.D., Member,
California Health Facilities Commission and Allan B. Caldwell, M.D.,
Gladden V. Elliott, M.D and Thomas Elmendorf, M.D., all members
of the Advisory Council. In addition, the question should be
considered in the light of the fact that all of these doctors earn
virtually all of their medical incomes by virtue of the hospital
contracts and are thus dependent on the operation of that hospital.
Although they are able to bring in patients who are not otherwise
admitted, it is not usually done.

     I believe in light of our time requirements, an informal opinion
would be sufficient.

     The second question asked whether the State of California had an
obligation to provide legal defense to members of the Commission,
its Advisory Council or staff if there are accusations of violations
of the Fair Political Practices Act. Your citation of Government
Codes 825 and 995 satisfies my question. I therefore withdraw
that question.

     Thank you for your attention.

                                        Yours truly,

                                        [signature]

                                        RONALD KALDOR
                                        General Counsel

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

CALIFORNIA HEALTH FACILITIES COMMISSION
555 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-2810


                                                    January 6, 1977


Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
925 J Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, California  95814


Dear Chairman Lowenstein:

     During the course of the preparation of the Conflict of Interest
Code for the California Health Facilities Commission, members
of the Advisory Council and the Commission have raised two
important questions of law.  I have been directed to request
legal opinions from your staff regarding them.

1.  Situation:

     A physician, who is in a designated position in the Conflict
     of Interest Code, acting as a corporation, contracts with a
     hospital to provide services to patients.  In the case
     of Radiology and Pathology, particularly, the contract
     prevents any other physicians from providing that service
     to patients in that facility.  The physician receives
     income from the service provided.  That income may either
     pass through the hospital books if the hospital performs
     the billing service or it may come directly to the medical
     corporation through direct billing to the patient.  In
     either case the income is considered the professional fee
     for the service rendered to the patient and not consideration
     from the contract with the hospital.

     Questions:

     a.  Does the physician have a financial interest in
         that hospital?

     b.  If that financial interest is required to be
         reported by the Conflict of Interest Code, is
         the value of the interest:

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein                -2-                January 6, 1977


     1) the income received as a result of the contract
        with the hospital;
     2) the income received by the corporation for
        professional services rendered at the hospital; or
     3) the portion of the income in 2) which is earned
        by the doctor designated in the Conflict of
        Interest Code?

2.  Situation:

     The members of the Commission or the Advisory Council or
     employees of the Commission comply in good faith with the
     Conflict of Interest Code adopted by the FPPC and other
     applicable conflict of interest laws.  Legal action is
     taken against them in spite of this good faith compliance.

     Question:

     Does the State of California have an obligation to provide
     legal defense to them as public officials?  If so, could
     that defense be performed by counsel to the Commission?

                                        Yours truly,

                                        [signature]

                                        RONALD KALDOR
                                        General Counsel

State of California

Memorandum

To      : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                    Date  :May 5, 1977


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION - Del Spurlock

Subject: Kaldor Letter


     Apparently this draft response has been discussed at the Opinion
Request Meeting. It appears to have some conceptual gaps or
problems in the area of the treatment of source of income.
It should be modified considerably before being sent out.


DLS:mfa

sections.question_synthetic: Whether physicians with exclusive service contracts at hospitals have reportable financial interests in those hospitals, and if so, how to value such interests; and whether the State must provide legal defense to Commission members and employees who face legal action despite good faith compliance with conflict of interest laws.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming request letter dated January 6, 1977, from Ronald Kaldor requesting legal opinions. The document includes a follow-up letter and internal FPPC meeting notes indicating the opinion had been drafted but required modification. No formal FPPC conclusion/advice letter is included in this document.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the California Health Facilities Commission seeking FPPC legal opinions on two questions regarding conflict of interest reporting for physicians with hospital contracts and state indemnification obligations for Commission members.
